name: AI Evals for Product Managers
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in AI Evals for Product Managers as a competency should be able to explain the role and importance of AI evaluations in product development, distinguish between offline, online, and human evals, and connect model metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, latency, etc.) to product and business outcomes (task completion, CSAT). They can frame evaluation objectives from product goals, define clear success criteria, basic hypotheses, and understand evaluation needs at different product lifecycle stages. They can design simple evaluation tasks and select appropriate metrics for common AI use cases (retrieval, classification, generation, summarization, recommendation, extraction), including both objective and subjective metrics, and understand tradeoffs (precision vs recall, quality vs latency/cost). They know how to build representative test datasets, include edge cases, avoid data leakage, and design scenario-based tests with basic sample sizing. They can set up and use simple human evaluation rubrics (pass/fail, rating scales), recognize rater bias and reliability issues, and interpret qualitative results for product decisions. They understand the basics of online evaluation (A/B tests, control vs treatment, guardrails, sample size), participate in experiment design, and interpret high-level results. They can categorize model errors, prioritize issues, and use evaluation insights to recommend product changes or launch decisions, while communicating uncertainty and limitations. They are aware of risk, safety, fairness, compliance, and privacy considerations, ensure safety checks and diverse test data, and escalate high-risk findings. They collaborate with DS/Eng, UX, and domain experts on evaluation design and execution, align stakeholders on plans and results, and manage expectations. They use basic tools (spreadsheets, forms, simple dashboards), understand evaluation platforms (OpenAI Evals, LangSmith, Weights & Biases prompts), and ensure traceability/versioning of eval runs. They document evaluation plans, results, and artifacts clearly, produce simple stakeholder reports, maintain up-to-date test sets and rubrics, share learnings, and ensure reproducibility and alignment between documentation and product behavior.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core Understanding of AI Evals in Product Context","scope_included":"- Understands the role of AI evaluations in product development
  - Can explain why traditional software QA is insufficient for AI features (probabilistic outputs, non-determinism, subjective quality)
  - Recognizes the differences between model performance metrics (e.g., accuracy) and product/business outcomes (e.g., task completion, CSAT)
  - Understands that evaluation is continuous (pre-launch, launch, and post-launch), not a one-time activity
  - Can articulate how evals reduce risk (quality, safety, regulatory, brand risk) before and after release
  - Distinguishes between:
    - Offline evals (test sets, benchmarks)
    - Online evals (A/B tests, feature flags)
    - Human evaluation (raters, domain experts, users)
  - Understands that Product Managers do not have to build models, but must own:
    - Defining success for AI features
    - Ensuring appropriate eval strategy
    - Making trade-off decisions using eval results

- Understands common AI product patterns and their evaluation implications
  - Retrieval-based assistants / chatbots
  - Classification / routing workflows
  - Content generation (e.g., marketing copy, email drafts)
  - Summarization (e.g., support ticket summaries, meeting notes)
  - Recommendation / ranking (e.g., knowledge articles, content feeds)
  - Form-filling / extraction (e.g., from PDFs, emails)

- Understands basic evaluation taxonomy
  - Objective metrics (e.g., accuracy, precision/recall, latency)
  - Subjective metrics (e.g., helpfulness, relevance, tone)
  - Constraint metrics (e.g., safety, policy compliance, PII leakage)
  - Product metrics (e.g., conversion, time-to-resolution, retention)

- Recognizes evaluation limitations
  - Overfitting to test sets
  - Misalignment between proxy metrics and real user value
  - Sampling bias in evaluation data
  - The need to periodically refresh test sets to combat model and environment drift","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Problem Framing and Evaluation Objectives","scope_included":"- Can translate product goals for an AI feature into evaluation objectives
  - Starts from business and user goals (e.g., reduce support handle time, improve self-serve resolutions)
  - Breaks goals down into measurable outcomes and evaluation questions ("How will we know if this AI assistant is good enough?")
  - Identifies primary vs secondary success criteria (e.g., quality first, then cost/latency)

- Defines clear evaluation hypotheses and thresholds
  - Formulates basic hypotheses (e.g., "AI-generated replies will reduce average handle time by 10% without lowering CSAT")
  - Works with DS/Eng to propose realistic performance targets (e.g., minimum acceptable accuracy, quality scores)
  - Differentiates between:
    - Acceptable launch thresholds
    - Aspirational performance goals for later iterations

- Aligns evaluation scope with product lifecycle stage
  - Early exploration: feasibility checks and rough quality assessments
  - Pre-launch: gate criteria for quality, safety, and UX fit
  - Post-launch: monitoring and regression detection
  - Understands which questions must be answered before shipping vs can be validated post-launch

- Identifies constraints that impact eval design
  - Data availability and labeling costs
  - Available human rater capacity
  - Latency and cost targets
  - Compliance or policy constraints (e.g., content policies, PII handling)

- Prioritizes what to measure given limited capacity
  - Separates "must-measure" from "nice-to-measure" metrics
  - Understands tradeoffs between breadth (many scenarios) and depth (many samples per scenario)
  - Can define a minimal but meaningful evaluation set for v1","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Task and Metric Design for Common AI Use Cases","scope_included":"- Can define evaluation tasks aligned to common AI product patterns
  - For retrieval/chatbots: defines tasks like "answer factual questions", "guide a troubleshooting flow", "route to correct article"
  - For summarization: defines tasks like "generate customer-friendly summary", "highlight key decisions and action items"
  - For generation: defines tasks like "draft email", "produce product description", "rewrite for a target tone"
  - For classification/routing: defines tasks like "assign category", "detect escalation", "identify spam or abuse"

- Selects and interprets basic metrics for these tasks
  - Quality metrics (task-specific)
    - Correctness / factual accuracy (for QA, classification)
    - Relevance (for retrieval, search, recommendations)
    - Completeness / coverage (for summarization)
    - Fluency / readability (for generation)
    - Formatting and structure adherence (for form filling, JSON output)
  - Efficiency metrics
    - Response latency
    - Steps-to-completion or clicks-to-completion
  - User-centric metrics
    - Task success rate (user resolved issue, completed action)
    - CSAT / NPS impact for AI-assisted flows

- Knows how to use basic metric types (conceptual, not necessarily implementational)
  - Exact match / accuracy for clearly right-or-wrong outputs
  - Top-K accuracy / hit rate for retrieval-based tasks
  - Simple string/semantic similarity metrics (high-level understanding, even if not implementing them)
  - Rating scales (e.g., 1–5 for helpfulness, 0–2 for correctness)

- Designs simple pass/fail and rating-based criteria
  - Can specify what constitutes a "pass" for a test case
  - Defines simple rating rubrics (e.g., 0 = incorrect, 1 = partially correct, 2 = fully correct)
  - Understands non-binary acceptability (good, acceptable, unacceptable) and how it maps to launch decisions

- Understands basic tradeoffs between metrics
  - Precision vs recall tradeoff in classification or detection tasks
  - Quality vs latency vs cost for generative tasks
  - Experience impact of false positives vs false negatives in the specific product context

- Avoids common metric design mistakes
  - Using only model-side metrics that users do not care about
  - Choosing metrics that are ambiguous or hard for raters to apply consistently
  - Overusing a single metric when multiple dimensions matter (e.g., quality and safety)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Test Data and Scenario Design","scope_included":"- Designs basic evaluation datasets from product reality
  - Can describe what a good test set should cover for their product
  - Works with DS/Eng/Operations to collect representative examples (from logs, support tickets, real user queries)
  - Identifies and includes edge cases and difficult scenarios (e.g., long queries, multi-step tasks, ambiguous questions)

- Ensures basic coverage across key dimensions
  - Core use cases and most frequent intents
  - High-value, high-risk, and high-volume scenarios
  - Different user segments or roles, when relevant
  - Different content types or languages, when in scope

- Understands basic dataset quality principles
  - Avoids data leakage between training and test sets (at least conceptually; ensures team checks this)
  - Recognizes and calls out obvious sampling biases (e.g., only recent data, only one customer segment)
  - Understands the need for clean, properly anonymized examples for evaluation

- Designs simple scenario-based tests
  - Writes clear test prompts or user queries from real workflows
  - Can outline realistic multi-turn interactions for conversational agents
  - Includes contextual information needed for the model (e.g., user profile, previous messages, references)

- Sizes evaluation samples at a basic level
  - Understands that more examples increase confidence in results, but cost more time and money
  - Can work with data partners to define a basic minimum sample size per scenario (even if not computing statistical power)

- Maintains and evolves test sets
  - Understands that test sets must be refreshed as products, content, or user behavior change
  - Can propose when to add new scenarios (e.g., new feature flows, emerging user intents)
  - Supports establishing tiers: smoke tests, core regression set, extended eval set","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Qualitative Evaluation and Rubric-Based Human Evals","scope_included":"- Designs basic human evaluation frameworks
  - Knows when human-in-the-loop evaluation is needed (e.g., for subjective quality, brand tone, nuanced correctness)
  - Defines evaluation questions raters should answer (e.g., "Is the answer factually correct?" "Is the tone appropriate?")
  - Uses simple rating scales (binary pass/fail, 3-point, or 5-point scales) with clear definitions

- Creates clear, usable rubrics for raters
  - Provides examples of good, borderline, and bad outputs
  - Specifies what to check for each dimension (e.g., correctness, relevance, completeness, tone, safety)
  - Clarifies what should be considered a blocking issue vs minor issue

- Understands basic reliability and bias issues
  - Recognizes that raters may be inconsistent; encourages using multiple raters for important evals
  - Understands that task instructions impact ratings and pushes for pilot tests
  - Identifies obvious sources of bias (e.g., only internal staff rating, unclear or leading instructions)

- Runs basic qualitative studies
  - Coordinates small-scale rating tasks with internal users or support agents
  - Uses structured templates (spreadsheets, forms, or simple platforms) to capture ratings and comments
  - Collects free-form feedback on failure modes and confusing behavior

- Interprets qualitative evaluation outputs for product decisions
  - Looks at rating distributions and central tendency (e.g., average rating, proportion of passes)
  - Summarizes common failure modes and user pain points
  - Uses rater feedback to inform prompt changes, feature constraints, or UX adjustments

- Balances qualitative and quantitative signals
  - Knows qualitative evals give depth and nuance; quantitative gives breadth and confidence
  - Uses qualitative f...
